original_hypothesis.title: AgentFactoryV4 execute path leaves custom-token application mappings uncleared, creating stuck tokenAddr state and potential fund recovery issues
original_hypothesis.hypothesis_description: In AgentFactoryV4, when a custom token application is executed, _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] and _applicationToken[id] are not cleared (they are only cleared on withdraw). This can permanently block re-applying with the same token address (because _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] != 0), even if the prior application is Executed and no longer needs the mapping. It also complicates recovery if execution partially fails after transfers/approvals but before completion.
original_hypothesis.investigation_rationale: This is a state machine correctness defect affecting protocol usability and potentially trapping user deposits if there are failure modes during custom token execution. It is attacker-relevant because griefers can intentionally create an application with a token to block others from using it again (if mapping is never cleared).

proof_of_signal.pattern: Mappings _tokenApplication/_applicationToken are cleared in withdraw() only; _executeApplication does not clear them on execution

preconditions: Custom token application created via initFromToken
Application is executed (status set to Executed)
Later attempts to reuse tokenAddr or recover mapping depend on these mappings

initial_code_evidence.vulnerable_pattern: stale bidirectional mapping not cleared on terminal state transition
initial_code_evidence.specific_concern: tokenAddr can become permanently reserved by first executor
initial_code_evidence.missing_safeguards: no cleanup on Executed; no admin recovery function

analysis_questions: Is the mapping intended to be a permanent uniqueness constraint (one agent per token forever), or only to prevent concurrent applications?
Can an attacker reserve popular tokens by applying/executing once, preventing others from using them?
Does any off-chain logic assume mappings are cleared on execution?
validation_requirements: Confirm state after executeTokenApplication: mapping values remain non-zero
Assess whether this blocks legitimate flows (e.g., retries after failure, reboots, migrations)
Determine if there is an intended alternative cleanup path

original_hypothesis.preliminary_attack_scenario: Attacker submits and executes a custom-token application with a tokenAddr that others want to use, permanently setting _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] to a non-zero id and blocking future initFromToken calls for that token.
prerequisites: Attacker can fund applicationThreshold and initialLP

potential_impact_if_confirmed.financial_impact: Moderate: ecosystem-level griefing and application pipeline disruption; potential stranded funds if failures occur mid-execution
potential_impact_if_confirmed.affected_users: Teams/users attempting to bootstrap agents using existing ERC20 tokens
potential_impact_if_confirmed.recovery_possibility: Medium if admin can upgrade/patch; otherwise blocked permanently for that factory instance

original_hypothesis.test_coverage_gap: No tests asserting mappings lifecycle across execute vs withdraw

technical_investigation_focus.detection_analysis: Monitor mapping values and NewPersona/NewApplication events

validation_result.mechanism: stale bidirectional mapping on execute path
validation_result.impact_summary: After executeTokenApplication completes, _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] and _applicationToken[id] remain set, permanently blocking initFromToken for that token address. This is inconsistent with the withdraw path which properly clears both mappings.
validation_result.detailed_analysis: The hypothesis correctly identifies that AgentFactoryV4._executeApplication does not clear the _tokenApplication and _applicationToken mappings upon successful execution, while the withdraw function does. This creates stale state after execution that permanently reserves the token address in the mapping, preventing any future initFromToken call with the same token address. While _createPair also prevents reuse by checking for existing pairs, the stale mapping is an independent defect that would persist even if the assetToken is changed (which would allow a new pair to be created, but the mapping check would still block initFromToken). The hypothesis's concern about partial execution failures is not valid since Solidity transactions are atomic (reverts undo all state changes). However, the core defect—inconsistent state cleanup between withdraw and execute paths—is a genuine code-level bug.
validation_result.code_evidence_assessment: Direct code inspection confirms the defect. In _executeApplication (lines ~216-290 of AgentFactoryV4.sol), after setting application.status = ApplicationStatus.Executed, the function proceeds through token creation, LP creation, veToken creation, DAO creation, NFT minting, TBA creation, and staking, but never touches _tokenApplication or _applicationToken. Meanwhile, withdraw (lines ~176-196) explicitly clears both: `_tokenApplication[customToken] = 0; _applicationToken[id] = address(0);`. The initFromToken function (lines ~454-516) checks `require(_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0, "Token already exists")`, which will permanently fail for any previously executed token.

vulnerable_code_snippets.vulnerability_explanation: The function transitions the application to Executed state and consumes the custom token for LP creation, but never clears _tokenApplication[tokenAddr] or _applicationToken[id]. This leaves stale state that permanently blocks initFromToken for that token address.
The withdraw path correctly clears both mappings, demonstrating the intended cleanup pattern that is missing from _executeApplication.
The guard `_tokenApplication[tokenAddr] == 0` will permanently fail for any token address that was previously used in an executed application, since _executeApplication never resets this mapping.

calling_sequence: 1. User A calls initFromToken(tokenX, ...) → sets _tokenApplication[tokenX] = id, _applicationToken[id] = tokenX
2. User A (or WITHDRAW_ROLE) calls executeTokenApplication(id, canStake) → calls _executeApplication(id, canStake, _tokenSupplyParams)
3. _executeApplication reads _applicationToken[id] = tokenX, creates pair, adds liquidity, creates veToken/DAO/NFT/TBA, stakes LP
5. If admin calls setAssetToken(newAssetToken), a new pair could be created for tokenX + newAssetToken
6. User B calls initFromToken(tokenX, ...) → REVERTS with 'Token already exists' because _tokenApplication[tokenX] != 0
execution_path_analysis.vulnerable_code_path: The _executeApplication function (AgentFactoryV4.sol lines 216-290) transitions the application status to Executed and uses the custom token, but the bidirectional mapping _tokenApplication/applicationToken is never cleared. The withdraw function (lines 176-196) properly clears these mappings with `_tokenApplication[customToken] = 0` and `_applicationToken[id] = address(0)`. This inconsistency means executed custom-token applications permanently reserve the token address in the factory's state.

config_validation.deployment_evidence: No deployment scripts provided for AgentFactoryV4 specifically. The personaFactoryArguments.js shows AgentFactory (V2) deployment but not V4. The defect is inherent in the code logic regardless of deployment configuration.

validation_result.attack_scenario_validation: The griefing scenario described in the hypothesis is valid but costly: an attacker must deposit applicationThreshold of assetToken plus initialLP of the target token, then get the application executed. After execution, the token address is permanently reserved in the _tokenApplication mapping. This blocks anyone from using initFromToken with that token address again on this factory. The scenario where assetToken is later changed by admin (making _createPair no longer a blocking factor) makes the stale mapping the sole barrier. However, the cost to the attacker (applicationThreshold + initialLP) and the requirement for execution (proposer or WITHDRAW_ROLE) limit the attack's practicality. The hypothesis's claim about 'stuck tokenAddr state' is fully confirmed by the code.
validation_result.impact_assessment: MEDIUM severity. The stale mapping after execution is a code defect that creates inconsistent state. Primary impacts: (1) Token address permanently reserved in _tokenApplication mapping after execution, preventing future initFromToken calls for that token, (2) Inconsistent state management between withdraw and execute code paths, (3) If assetToken is later changed by admin, legitimate users cannot create new applications with previously-executed custom tokens. The defect does not cause direct fund loss but degrades protocol functionality and could block legitimate use cases over time.
recommended_mitigations: Clear both mappings in _executeApplication after the custom token has been consumed for LP creation: add `_tokenApplication[token] = 0; _applicationToken[id] = address(0);` after the liquidity addition block in _executeApplication
Alternatively, if the intent is permanent uniqueness, document this clearly and ensure the _createPair check is the canonical guard rather than the mapping
Consider using a different sentinel value (e.g., type(uint256).max) instead of 0 for the _tokenApplication mapping to avoid collision with application id 0 if _nextId starts at 0
validation_result.replication_plan: 1. Deploy AgentFactoryV4 with nextId_ > 0. 2. Call initFromToken with a valid ERC20 token (tokenX) and sufficient funds. 3. Call executeTokenApplication for the created application ID. 4. Verify _tokenApplication[tokenX] != 0 (still set). 5. Attempt initFromToken with tokenX again, expect revert 'Token already exists'. 6. Change assetToken via setAssetToken. 7. Attempt initFromToken with tokenX again, still expect revert 'Token already exists' despite new pair being possible.
uncertainties: Whether the protocol design intentionally prevents reuse of a custom token address (permanent one-agent-per-token policy) or this is an oversight
Whether the _nextId parameter in V4 initialize is always set to a non-zero value in production deployments (affects sentinel value collision)
additional_testing_needed: Verify the actual nextId_ value used in V4 deployment to confirm whether id=0 sentinel collision is possible
Test the full lifecycle: initFromToken → executeTokenApplication → verify mapping state → attempt re-registration
Test the assetToken change scenario to confirm the stale mapping is the sole barrier to reuse
